danger_signals.visual_form: Player animations showing shoving/elbowing during play
danger_signals.meaning: Opponent is actively contesting possession; no foul penalty exists

danger_signals.visual_form: Opponent positioning close to player character
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat to ball possession or shooting attempt

danger_signals.visual_form: On-screen timer countdown (inferred from rule system)
danger_signals.meaning: Possession will be lost if shot not attempted within time window

safety_signals.visual_form: Large uncontested areas on court between player and basket
safety_signals.meaning: Opportunity for fast break or uncontested scoring attempt

safety_signals.visual_form: AI teammate character placement relative to player
safety_signals.meaning: Safe passing option available; allied presence reduces isolation risk

safety_signals.visual_form: Spatial separation between player character and opposing team
safety_signals.meaning: Safe window for shooting or dribbling without immediate contest

faction_indicators.color_identity: Team-specific jersey colors (1992–93 or 1993–94 NBA rosters)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: NBA team logos and names (implied on jerseys and scoreboard)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Away team renders in alternate jersey coloration
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Home court branding visible on court/sidelines (inferred)

faction_indicators.color_identity: Secondary/alternate jersey palette per NBA team
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Distinct visual differentiation from home team through color contrast
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Away uniform designation; opposing team sideline representation (inferred)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character rendering changes to indicate hot status; visual effect emphasizes player state
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Unlimited turbo availability; increased shooting precision; continued scoring extends fire status until opponent scores or player scores 4 additional baskets while on fire

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ball rendered in player's hands; player character highlighted as active controller
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can move, dribble, pass, or shoot; turbo meter available for use

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ball held by opponent character model; defensive player becomes focal point
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player switches to defensive positioning; must contest opponent shooting/passing without foul penalty available

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: 24-second timer visually decrements on-screen (inferred from rule system)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Team must attempt shot or lose possession; time pressure escalates late in clock window

environmental_storytelling.example: Real NBA players from 1992–93 season (arcade) or 1993–94 season (console) rendered as digitized sprites, creating visual connection to actual athletes and teams; absence of Michael Jordan and Shaquille O'Neal (licensing limitations) visually marks their unavailability
NBA team logos, court colors, and sideline aesthetics embed team identity into play environment; home/away jersey differentiation signals faction alignment
Extreme dunking heights and impossible jump trajectories visually communicate game's arcade style and power fantasy, differentiating it from realistic sports sims

Hidden character unlocks (Bill Clinton, Hugo the mascot, secret players) and special features (Super Clean Floors) create discoverable world-states that alter visual behavior and character availability, rewarding player exploration

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Unlimited turbo; increased shooting precision; animation/visual state changes to indicate hot status

entity_behavioral_tells.state: On fire, post-scoring (4 additional consecutive baskets)
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Fire status expires; turbo returns to limited meter; shooting precision normalizes
Can freely shove or elbow opponents (no foul penalty); can dribble, pass, or shoot; turbo meter enables enhanced movement
Can contest shots and passes; uses physical contact without foul consequences; positioning indicates defensive pressure
Must attempt shot before clock expires or possession is lost; behavioral urgency increases as timer depletes
Characters fall if running too fast or changing direction too quickly; physics behavior visually altered to indicate special rule state

notes: NBA Jam: Tournament Edition operates within a sports arcade paradigm that prioritizes spectacle and accessibility over simulation realism. Danger and safety signals emerge primarily from spatial relationships (defender proximity, court openness) and possession state rather than explicit visual warnings. Faction identity is rooted in NBA team authenticity—real team rosters, colors, and likenesses create sports legitimacy. The 'on fire' mechanic is the primary world-state indicator, signaling momentum and player performance visually and mechanically. Easter eggs and hidden features (unlockable characters, special modes like Super Clean Floors) represent discoverable world-state variations. No explicit political or ideological faction systems exist; team differentiation is purely sports-competitive. The absence of fouls/free throws/violations simplifies visual communication—no penalty animation states clutter the screen, focusing player attention on action and scoring. Digitized player graphics anchor the game to real 1990s NBA identity, though licensing gaps (Jordan, O'Neal) are visible absences that communicate commercial/legal constraints as narrative gaps.
